quiz_questions.question_text: How many characteristics must something display to be considered alive?

options.text: All of them — living things must show every characteristic of life
At least three out of seven
Only one — any single characteristic is enough
Only if it moves on its own

quiz_questions.explanation: To be considered alive, an organism must display ALL the characteristics of life: organization, metabolism, homeostasis, growth, reproduction, response to stimuli, and adaptation. Showing just one or a few of them is not enough.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of these is NOT a characteristic of life?

options.text: Being made of metal
Reproduction
Homeostasis
Metabolism

quiz_questions.explanation: The characteristics of life include reproduction, homeostasis, and metabolism — but being made of metal is not one of them. Living things are made of cells, not metal.

quiz_questions.question_text: All living things are made of:

options.text: Only molecules
Organs
Bones and muscles
One or more cells

quiz_questions.explanation: Cellular organization is a key characteristic of life. Every living thing, from bacteria to whales, is made of at least one cell. Not all living things have organs, bones, or muscles.

quiz_questions.question_text: Sweating on a hot day to cool your body down is an example of:

options.text: Adaptation
Reproduction
Homeostasis
Growth

quiz_questions.explanation: Homeostasis is the process of maintaining stable internal conditions. Sweating cools your body back to its normal temperature — a classic example of homeostasis.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which characteristic of life refers to ALL the chemical reactions in an organism?

options.text: Homeostasis
Response to stimuli
Metabolism
Reproduction

quiz_questions.explanation: Metabolism is the sum of all the chemical reactions that happen in an organism, including breaking down food for energy and building new molecules.

quiz_questions.question_text: A plant turning toward sunlight is an example of:

options.text: Reproduction
Growth
Response to stimuli
Homeostasis

quiz_questions.explanation: When a plant turns toward light, it is reacting to an environmental change (a stimulus). This is called response to stimuli — one of the characteristics of life.

quiz_questions.question_text: Over millions of years, a species of fish developed thicker scales to resist parasites. This is an example of:

options.text: Growth
Adaptation
Homeostasis
Metabolism

quiz_questions.explanation: Adaptation is a change in a population over many generations that helps organisms survive in their environment. Thicker scales evolving over millions of years to resist parasites is adaptation through natural selection.

quiz_questions.question_text: A kitten grows into an adult cat. This increase in size and complexity is called:

options.text: Adaptation
Homeostasis
Growth and development
Metabolism

quiz_questions.explanation: Growth and development is the characteristic of life that describes organisms increasing in size and becoming more complex according to their genetic instructions.

quiz_questions.question_text: Is fire considered alive? It 'grows,' 'consumes' fuel, and 'reproduces' (spreads).

options.text: No — fire is not made of cells and lacks most characteristics of life
Yes — it meets enough characteristics
Yes — if it uses energy, it is alive
It depends on how hot the fire is

quiz_questions.explanation: Fire is NOT alive. Although it can mimic some characteristics of life, it is not made of cells, cannot truly reproduce, does not maintain homeostasis, and does not have DNA. To be alive, ALL characteristics must be present.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the difference between sexual and asexual reproduction?

options.text: There is no real difference
Sexual is only for animals; asexual is only for plants
Sexual involves two parents; asexual involves one parent
Sexual is faster; asexual is slower

quiz_questions.explanation: Sexual reproduction requires two parents and produces genetically unique offspring. Asexual reproduction requires only one parent and produces genetically identical copies (clones). Both plants and animals can use either method.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'homeostasis'?

options.text: A change in a species over millions of years
The process of growing larger over time
The ability to produce offspring
The maintenance of stable internal conditions despite external changes

quiz_questions.explanation: Homeostasis is when organisms keep their internal environment stable — like maintaining body temperature, blood sugar, or water balance — even when outside conditions change.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'metabolism'?

options.text: The process of maintaining stable body temperature
The passing of traits from parent to offspring
All the chemical reactions within an organism that sustain life
The ability to react to the environment

quiz_questions.explanation: Metabolism refers to every chemical reaction in a living organism — including breaking down food for energy (catabolism) and building new molecules (anabolism). It keeps organisms alive and functioning.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'reproduction' mean in biology?

options.text: The process of growing larger
Breaking down food for energy
Reacting to a change in the environment
The biological process by which organisms produce offspring

quiz_questions.explanation: Reproduction is the process by which living organisms produce new individuals (offspring). It can be sexual (two parents) or asexual (one parent).

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'growth and development' mean as a characteristic of life?

options.text: Learning new skills over time
Moving to a new habitat
An increase in size and complexity following genetic instructions
Getting stronger by exercising

quiz_questions.explanation: Growth means an increase in size (more or bigger cells). Development means increasing complexity (a single cell becomes a baby with specialized organs). Both follow the instructions in the organism's DNA.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'response to stimuli' mean?

options.text: The sum of all chemical reactions in a cell
The ability to maintain a constant body temperature
The ability of organisms to react to environmental changes
The process of producing new organisms

quiz_questions.explanation: Response to stimuli means living things detect and react to changes in their environment. For example, pulling your hand from a hot surface, a plant turning toward light, or a dog salivating at the smell of food.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'adaptation' in biology?

options.text: The process of cell division
An organism getting used to cold weather in one winter
A chemical reaction inside a cell
A trait that helps an organism survive, developed over many generations through natural selection

quiz_questions.explanation: In biology, adaptation refers to inherited traits that improve survival and reproduction, developed over many generations through natural selection. It is NOT the same as an individual adjusting to conditions within its lifetime.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'cellular organization'?

options.text: How organs are arranged in the body
The way files are organized on a computer
The fact that all living things are made of one or more cells
The structure of DNA molecules

quiz_questions.explanation: Cellular organization means all living things are composed of cells — the basic unit of life. Some organisms have just one cell (unicellular), while others have trillions (multicellular).

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'unicellular organism'?

options.text: An organism made of a single cell that performs all life functions
An organism with many specialized cells
A cell found only in universities
An organism that cannot reproduce

quiz_questions.explanation: Unicellular organisms (like bacteria and amoebas) consist of just one cell that carries out ALL life functions — metabolism, reproduction, homeostasis, and response to stimuli — all within that one cell.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'binary fission'?

options.text: The process of breaking down food for energy
A computer term for splitting data
A type of sexual reproduction in bacteria
A form of asexual reproduction where one cell splits into two identical cells

quiz_questions.explanation: Binary fission is how bacteria and other unicellular organisms reproduce asexually. The cell copies its DNA and splits in half, producing two genetically identical daughter cells.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'negative feedback' in biology?

options.text: The opposite of metabolism
A process that always makes things worse
A process where the body counteracts a change to maintain stability
Negative criticism from one organism to another

quiz_questions.explanation: Negative feedback is a homeostatic mechanism where the body's response counteracts (reverses) a change. For example: blood sugar rises → insulin is released → blood sugar drops back to normal. The response opposes the change, maintaining balance.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student places a drop of pond water under a microscope and sees a tiny green organism moving toward a light source. Which characteristics of life does this organism demonstrate?

options.text: Only metabolism — it uses light for energy
None — microscopic things are not alive
Only response to stimuli — cells are too small to count
Cellular organization and response to stimuli

quiz_questions.explanation: The organism is made of at least one cell (cellular organization — visible under a microscope) and moves toward light (response to stimuli — called phototaxis). Microscopic organisms are absolutely alive.

quiz_questions.question_text: On a hot day, you start sweating. When your temperature returns to normal, you stop sweating. A friend says this means your body is 'adapting' to the heat. Is your friend correct?

options.text: Yes — sweating means your body is evolving in real time
Yes — any response to the environment is adaptation
No — this is homeostasis (maintaining internal stability), not adaptation (which takes generations)
No — sweating is metabolism, not homeostasis or adaptation

quiz_questions.explanation: Sweating is homeostasis — a short-term mechanism to keep body temperature stable. Adaptation is an evolutionary change over many generations, not something that happens in an afternoon. While metabolism IS involved in producing sweat, the regulatory function described is specifically homeostasis.

quiz_questions.question_text: COVID-19 is caused by SARS-CoV-2, which can mutate and spread but cannot replicate without entering a human cell. Based on the characteristics of life, viruses are BEST classified as:

options.text: Non-living like a rock — they have no biological properties at all
Fully living — they can mutate and evolve like living things
Not fully living — they lack independent reproduction and metabolism
Unicellular organisms — they are made of one cell

quiz_questions.explanation: Viruses exist on the boundary of life. They have genetic material and can evolve, but they cannot reproduce or carry out metabolism independently — they must hijack a host cell. They are NOT cells, and they do have biological properties (unlike a rock), so they are 'not fully living.'

quiz_questions.question_text: A student argues: 'A car uses energy, responds to the driver, and is 'born' in a factory. Therefore, cars are alive.' What is the BEST counterargument?

options.text: Cars are made of metal, and metal is never part of living things
The student is correct — cars meet enough characteristics to be alive
Cars lack cellular organization, cannot reproduce on their own, and do not grow or evolve
Cars use gasoline, and living things only use food

quiz_questions.explanation: To be alive, ALL characteristics of life must be present. Cars are not made of cells, cannot reproduce independently, do not grow biologically, and do not evolve. Some living things DO contain metals (iron in hemoglobin), and the fuel type doesn't matter — cellular organization and self-replication are the key differences.

quiz_questions.question_text: Peppered moths in England were originally light-colored. During the Industrial Revolution, pollution darkened tree bark and dark moths became more common over generations. A student says individual moths 'changed their color to blend in.' Why is this explanation WRONG?

options.text: Adaptation only happens to mammals, not insects
Moths can actually change their color like chameleons
Pollution doesn't affect organisms at all
Individual moths didn't change — dark moths survived better and passed on their genes to more offspring

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a common misconception. Adaptation through natural selection happens at the POPULATION level, not the individual level. Individual moths did not change color — dark-colored moths already existed and had a survival advantage on dark bark, so they reproduced more. Over generations, the population shifted to be mostly dark.

quiz_questions.question_text: Scientists discover organisms near deep-sea volcanic vents where there is no sunlight. These organisms get energy from chemical reactions with hydrogen sulfide. A student says they can't be alive because 'all life needs sunlight.' Is the student correct?

options.text: Yes — without sunlight, nothing can have cells
No — these organisms are not performing metabolism at all
No — all life needs energy, but it doesn't have to come from sunlight (these use chemosynthesis)
Yes — all living things ultimately depend on the sun

quiz_questions.explanation: All life needs energy, but NOT all life needs sunlight. Chemosynthetic organisms at hydrothermal vents use chemical energy from hydrogen sulfide instead of sunlight. They are fully alive — they have cells, reproduce, maintain homeostasis, and perform metabolism through chemosynthesis.

quiz_questions.question_text: A pregnant woman eats a meal, and her blood sugar rises. Her pancreas releases insulin, which lowers blood sugar back to normal. Later, her blood sugar drops too low, so her pancreas releases glucagon to raise it. Both processes together are an example of:

options.text: Adaptation — her body evolved to process sugar during pregnancy
Positive feedback because the body keeps making more hormones
Negative feedback loops maintaining homeostasis
Growth — the baby needs sugar to develop

quiz_questions.explanation: Both insulin (lowering high sugar) and glucagon (raising low sugar) are negative feedback mechanisms — they counteract changes to maintain blood sugar at a stable set point. This is homeostasis in action. Positive feedback amplifies changes instead of reversing them.

quiz_questions.question_text: A colony of bacteria doubles every 20 minutes through binary fission. A student says this is sexual reproduction because 'the cells are splitting into two.' Why is the student WRONG?

options.text: Bacteria are too small to reproduce
Splitting in two means both cells die
Sexual reproduction also involves splitting, so the student is correct
Binary fission is asexual — one cell copies its DNA and splits; no partner is needed

quiz_questions.explanation: Binary fission is asexual reproduction — one parent cell copies its DNA and splits into two identical daughter cells. Sexual reproduction requires TWO parents combining genetic material (like sperm and egg). The fact that a cell splits doesn't make it sexual — what matters is whether genetic material from two parents is combined.

quiz_questions.question_text: A scientist finds an organism in a cave that has no eyes and no pigmentation. Its ancestors had both. Over thousands of generations, these traits disappeared. A student asks, 'Did individual cave organisms choose to lose their eyes?' What is the correct explanation?

options.text: No — traits that offered no survival benefit were lost through natural selection over many generations
Eyes cannot be lost — the scientist is wrong
The organisms lost their eyes because of homeostasis
Yes — organisms can decide which traits to keep

quiz_questions.explanation: Individuals don't choose which traits to have. Over many generations, in total darkness, organisms with mutations reducing eyes didn't lose any survival advantage (eyes are useless in the dark) and may have saved energy. Through natural selection, the eyeless trait became common in the population. This is adaptation, not an individual choice or homeostasis.

quiz_questions.question_text: A pet goldfish always swims to the top of the tank when its owner approaches. The owner says the fish has 'adapted' to expect food. Is this the correct use of the word 'adaptation'?

options.text: No — this is response to stimuli (learned behavior within one lifetime), not adaptation (which takes many generations)
No — fish cannot learn anything
Yes — the fish evolved this behavior in captivity
Yes — any behavioral change is an adaptation

quiz_questions.explanation: In everyday language, 'adapt' means to adjust — but in biology, adaptation specifically means an inherited trait that evolved over many generations through natural selection. The goldfish learned to associate the owner with food within its lifetime — that's a response to stimuli (learned behavior), not biological adaptation. Fish CAN learn, but learning is not the same as evolving.
